wcag_dictionary.featherless_prompt_context: The user's help widget moves position across pages. Generate a shared layout component keeping the help mechanism in a consistent relative position across all page templates to satisfy WCAG 3.2.6.

wcag_dictionary.description: If an input error is automatically detected, the item that is in error is identified and the error is described to the user in text.
common_failures: Error state conveyed only by red border with no text
Error message not associated with the errored input
aria-invalid used on the field but no error message linked via aria-describedby
Error appears visually but is not announced to screen readers
wcag_dictionary.impact: Critical — users with cognitive disabilities and screen reader users need clear, text-based error descriptions to correct their inputs.
wcag_dictionary.test_procedure: Submit a form with invalid data. Verify each errored field is identified in text and the error message is programmatically associated with the field (e.g., via aria-describedby).
wcag_dictionary.featherless_prompt_context: The user's form shows errors only visually. Generate HTML with aria-invalid and aria-describedby linking the field to an accessible error message to satisfy WCAG 3.3.1.

wcag_dictionary.description: Labels or instructions are provided when content requires user input.
common_failures: Placeholder text used as the only label (disappears on focus)
Required field format not described (e.g., date format MM/DD/YYYY)
No label associated with an input field
Instructions provided only after the relevant form control
wcag_dictionary.impact: Serious — users with cognitive disabilities need clear instructions and labels to complete form fields correctly.
wcag_dictionary.test_procedure: Verify every form input has a persistent visible label (not placeholder-only) and that required format or constraints are described before the input.
wcag_dictionary.featherless_prompt_context: The user's form relies on placeholder text as the only label. Generate corrected HTML with a visible <label> element and optionally a format hint using aria-describedby to satisfy WCAG 3.3.2.

wcag_dictionary.description: If an input error is automatically detected and suggestions for correction are known, then the suggestion is provided to the user, unless it would jeopardize the security or purpose of the content.
common_failures: Error message says 'Invalid email' without specifying the expected format
Password error says 'Password too short' without stating the minimum length
Date field error says 'Invalid date' without showing the required format
Error only indicates the field is wrong with no guidance on how to fix it
wcag_dictionary.impact: Serious for users with cognitive disabilities, learning disabilities, and low literacy who need concrete, specific guidance to correct their mistakes.
wcag_dictionary.test_procedure: Trigger validation errors by submitting invalid data. Verify each error message includes a specific, actionable suggestion (e.g., 'Enter a valid email address, for example: name@example.com').
wcag_dictionary.featherless_prompt_context: The user's form shows generic error messages. Generate HTML with specific, accessible error messages linked via aria-describedby that include format examples and correction guidance to satisfy WCAG 3.3.3.

wcag_dictionary.description: For web pages that cause legal commitments or financial transactions for the user to occur, that modify or delete user-controllable data in data storage systems, or that submit user test responses, submissions are reversible, verified, or confirmed.
common_failures: One-click purchase with no order review or confirmation step
Deleting an account with no confirmation dialog and no undo
Submitting a legal form with no review page
Overwriting saved data with no warning or ability to cancel

requirements.reversible: Submissions can be reversed (e.g., a cancellation period for orders).
requirements.checked: Data entered by the user is checked for input errors and the user is given the opportunity to correct them.
requirements.confirmed: A mechanism is available for reviewing, confirming, and correcting information before finalising the submission.

wcag_dictionary.impact: Critical for users with cognitive disabilities, motor disabilities (who may accidentally submit), and any user performing high-stakes transactions.
wcag_dictionary.test_procedure: Identify all submission flows involving financial transactions, legal commitments, or data deletion. Verify at least one of: a review/confirm step, an undo mechanism, or a clearly labelled correction opportunity exists.
wcag_dictionary.featherless_prompt_context: The user's checkout or deletion flow has no confirmation step. Generate an HTML/JS snippet adding a review and confirm dialog before the final destructive or transactional action to satisfy WCAG 3.3.4.

wcag_dictionary.description: Context-sensitive help is available.
common_failures: Complex forms with no inline help or hint text
No tooltips, help icons, or contextual documentation on complex inputs
Help is only accessible via a general FAQ page with no direct context link
wcag_dictionary.impact: Serious for users with cognitive disabilities who need in-context guidance to complete complex tasks.
wcag_dictionary.test_procedure: Verify complex or non-obvious form fields and interactions provide context-sensitive help (tooltip, adjacent hint, help icon linking to instructions).
wcag_dictionary.featherless_prompt_context: The user's form has no context-sensitive help. Generate HTML adding help icons with aria-describedby linked tooltips or hint text adjacent to complex form fields to satisfy WCAG 3.3.5.

wcag_dictionary.description: For web pages that require the user to submit information, at least one of the following is true: submissions are reversible, data is checked and the user can correct errors, or a mechanism is available for reviewing, confirming, and correcting information before finalising submission.
common_failures: Any form submission with no review step, confirmation, or undo mechanism
Newsletter signup with no confirmation or unsubscribe option
Profile update with no review page and no undo
wcag_dictionary.impact: Stricter than 3.3.4 — applies to all form submissions, not just legal, financial, or data-critical ones.
wcag_dictionary.test_procedure: Test every form submission. Verify at least one safeguard exists: reversible, checkable, or confirmable.
wcag_dictionary.featherless_prompt_context: The user has a simple form with no error prevention safeguard. Generate an HTML confirmation step or summary screen before final submission to satisfy WCAG 3.3.6.

wcag_dictionary.description: Information previously entered by or provided to the user that is required to be entered again in the same process is either auto-populated or available for the user to select. (New in WCAG 2.2)
common_failures: Checkout flow asks for billing address after shipping address was already entered, with no auto-fill option
Multi-step form asks for the same information on different steps without pre-populating
Registration flow asks for email twice without indicating it will be verified
wcag_dictionary.note: New in WCAG 2.2. Exception: re-entering data is required for security (e.g., confirming a password) or when the previous entry is no longer valid.
wcag_dictionary.impact: Serious for users with cognitive disabilities and motor disabilities who find it difficult or burdensome to re-enter previously provided information.
wcag_dictionary.test_procedure: Walk through all multi-step processes. Identify any step that asks for information already provided. Verify it is pre-populated or selectable from previous input.
wcag_dictionary.featherless_prompt_context: The user's multi-step form repeats previously collected data. Generate JavaScript pre-populating repeated fields from earlier step data stored in session state to satisfy WCAG 3.3.7.

wcag_dictionary.description: A cognitive function test is not required for any step in an authentication process unless an alternative is provided, or the test is to recognise objects or personal content. (New in WCAG 2.2)
common_failures: CAPTCHA with no audio or object-recognition alternative
Password field that blocks paste, preventing use of password managers
Authentication requires memorising a code with no copy-paste option
Puzzle-based CAPTCHA with no accessible alternative
wcag_dictionary.note: New in WCAG 2.2. Cognitive function tests include remembering passwords, solving puzzles, and transcribing characters. Object recognition (select all images with a car) and personal content (recognise your own photo) are exempt.
wcag_dictionary.impact: Critical for users with cognitive disabilities who cannot solve memory or transcription-based authentication challenges.
wcag_dictionary.test_procedure: Review the authentication flow. Verify no step requires a cognitive function test without an alternative. Verify paste is not blocked on password or code fields.
wcag_dictionary.featherless_prompt_context: The user's login has a CAPTCHA or blocks clipboard paste. Generate an authentication flow using passkeys or object-recognition CAPTCHA alternatives and ensure password fields allow paste to satisfy WCAG 3.3.8.

wcag_dictionary.description: A cognitive function test is not required for any step in an authentication process. (New in WCAG 2.2)
common_failures: Any CAPTCHA with no alternative whatsoever
Object-recognition or personal content tests still required with no alternative
Authentication requires any memory or recognition task
wcag_dictionary.note: New in WCAG 2.2. Stricter than 3.3.8 — removes the object-recognition and personal-content exemptions. No cognitive function test of any kind is permitted.
wcag_dictionary.impact: Critical for users with the most severe cognitive impairments for whom even object recognition is a barrier.
wcag_dictionary.test_procedure: Verify the authentication process contains absolutely no cognitive function tests — no CAPTCHA of any type, no memory challenge, no object recognition.
wcag_dictionary.featherless_prompt_context: The user's authentication uses any cognitive test. Generate an authentication flow based entirely on passkeys, magic links, or hardware tokens with no cognitive challenge to satisfy WCAG 3.3.9.

wcag_dictionary.description: In content implemented using markup languages, elements have complete start and end tags, elements are nested according to their specifications, elements do not contain duplicate attributes, and IDs are unique.

act_rules.title: ID attribute value is unique

common_failures: Duplicate id attributes in the same document
Unclosed HTML tags
Improperly nested elements (e.g., <div> inside <span>)
Duplicate attributes on the same element
wcag_dictionary.impact: Serious — duplicate IDs break ARIA relationships (labelledby, describedby, controls), causing assistive technologies to reference the wrong element.
affected_users: Users of any assistive technology that relies on DOM structure
wcag_dictionary.test_procedure: Validate the HTML with the W3C Markup Validator. Check for duplicate IDs using browser DevTools or document.querySelectorAll('[id]').
wcag_dictionary.featherless_prompt_context: The user's page has duplicate IDs or invalid HTML structure. Generate a corrected HTML snippet with unique IDs and valid nesting to satisfy WCAG 4.1.1.

wcag_dictionary.description: For all user interface components, the name and role can be programmatically determined; states, properties, and values that can be set by the user can be programmatically set; and notification of changes to these items is available to user agents, including assistive technologies.

act_rules.title: Button has non-empty accessible name
Form field has non-empty accessible name
Iframe element has non-empty accessible name

common_failures: Custom <div> acting as a button without role='button'
Icon-only button with no accessible name
Custom checkbox/toggle without aria-checked state
<iframe> without a title attribute
Combobox without aria-expanded state
Form input without associated <label> or aria-label
wcag_dictionary.impact: Critical — assistive technologies cannot interpret custom or poorly-coded components without a programmatic name, role, and state.
wcag_dictionary.test_procedure: Open the browser Accessibility Tree (DevTools > Accessibility). Verify every interactive element shows a correct computed name, role, and state. Test dynamic state changes (e.g., expanded/collapsed) are updated in the tree.
wcag_dictionary.featherless_prompt_context: The user provided a custom UI component lacking a programmatic name or role. Generate an optimized code snippet adding appropriate ARIA roles, states, or labels to satisfy WCAG 4.1.2.

wcag_dictionary.description: In content implemented using markup languages, status messages can be programmatically determined through role or properties such that they can be presented to the user by assistive technologies without receiving focus.
common_failures: Success/error toast notifications displayed visually but not announced by screen readers
Live search result counts not in an aria-live region
Shopping cart update notification not announced
Form submission confirmation message not in a live region
wcag_dictionary.impact: Serious — screen reader users miss important status updates (success, error, loading complete) if they are not conveyed through live regions.

aria_roles.status: role='status' (polite) — for non-critical status messages like 'Item added to cart'
aria_roles.alert: role='alert' (assertive) — for critical messages like 'Error: session expired'
aria_roles.log: role='log' — for chat logs or cumulative status updates
aria_roles.progressbar: role='progressbar' with aria-valuenow/min/max for progress indicators

wcag_dictionary.test_procedure: Trigger status messages (form submit, search, cart update) while using a screen reader. Verify the message is announced without moving focus to it.
wcag_dictionary.featherless_prompt_context: The user has a toast notification or status update that is not announced by screen readers. Generate HTML with the appropriate role='status' or role='alert' and an aria-live region to satisfy WCAG 4.1.3.

conformance_levels.AA: Standard level — legally required in most jurisdictions

metadata.note: Complete WCAG 2.2 dictionary covering all Level A, AA, and AAA success criteria.
